title: Code-agnostic bosonic noise suppression with hybrid rotations
authors: Saurabh U. Shringarpure, Siheon Park, Sungjoo Cho, Yong Siah Teo, Hyukjoon Kwon, Srikrishna Omkar, Hyunseok Jeong
abstract: Physical-level noise on traveling bosonic modes remains a critical bottleneck for scalable quantum information processing. We show that for any single-mode bosonic code (qumode) corrupted by thermal or Gaussian displacement noise at loss rate $μ$ and amplification $G$, a hybrid continuous-discrete-variable (CV-DV) interferometer using a single qubit ancilla and two controlled-Fourier (CF) gates sandwiching the noise channel suppresses its effects from linear to quadratic scaling. This is achieved without active error correction or destructive measurements of the encoded state, maintaining high success probabilities $\geq 0.5$ when $μG \leq 0.5$. When supplemented with multiple ancillas, the protocol converts photon loss into coherent Fock-damping, and thermal or displacement noise into a mixture of Fock-diagonal noise. The protocol is entirely code-agnostic. For the special case of $2^K$-fold rotation-symmetric bosonic codes, it simplifies to conventional error detection and projection with $K$ ancillas. Suppression with simple gates and few ancillas demonstrates a clear hardware-efficient advantage over previously proposed ``bypass'' schemes, where quantum information transferred to the DV ancillas is readily corrupted by ancilla noise. Finally, we extend the protocol to a qutrit DV ancilla. This demonstrates resilience to both CV noise and composite DV damping noise, achieving a truly hybrid noise suppression scheme that operates effectively even on CV encodings lacking a well-defined photon-number parity syndrome.
summary: The paper introduces a code-agnostic protocol to suppress bosonic noise in traveling modes by using a hybrid CV-DV interferometer. By sandwiching noise between controlled-Fourier gates, the protocol reduces error scaling from linear to quadratic. The method is versatile, working for various bosonic codes and extending to qutrit ancillas to handle complex noise environments.
main_problem: Physical-level noise in traveling bosonic modes, such as photon loss, thermal excitation, and Gaussian displacement noise, acts as a critical bottleneck for scalable quantum information processing.
main_result: The authors propose a code-agnostic, hybrid CV-DV interferometer that suppresses noise effects from linear to quadratic scaling without active error correction or destructive measurements.
method: A feedback-free protocol using a single qubit (or qutrit) ancilla and two controlled-Fourier (CF) gates that sandwich the noise channel to induce destructive interference of error terms.
model_or_system: A hybrid continuous-variable (CV) and discrete-variable (DV) system, applicable to platforms like cavity/circuit QED and trapped ions, involving bosonic modes (qumodes) and qubit or qutrit ancillas.
key_observables: Success probability (p_succ), average fidelity (F), and infidelity (1-F).
important_parameters: Loss rate (mu), amplification gain (G), thermal noise rate (eta), mean photon number (n_bar), and ancilla damping strength (p).
main_assumptions: Assumes the ancilla is initialized; assumes noiseless CF gates for the ideal case; assumes the ancilla damping channels commute.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the hybrid interferometer setup and optical circuit implementation; Figure 2 illustrates the multi-ancilla extension; Figure 3 shows average suppression performance; Figure 4 compares parity-based code resilience; Figure 5 shows performance vs. ancilla damping; Figure 6 compares squeezed vs. bare cat codes; Figure 9/10 show the qutrit extension.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the noise problem, presents the core single-ancilla CF-gate protocol, extends the method to multi-ancilla and qutrit systems, analyzes performance for various bosonic codes (cat, GKP, binomial), and evaluates the protocol in quantum communication/teleportation scenarios.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and open quantum systems as it provides a hardware-efficient, passive method to reshape noise channels and improve the fidelity of bosonic encodings using hybrid CV-DV interactions.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Bosonic noise suppression with (a) conditional rota- tion (condrot) gates and (b) a series of compositions of conditional displacements and the conditional rotation gates. We shall refer to the unitary with L layers by the notation “[PQP-condrot]L” for convenience. The conditional rotation gate of (a) is optimally the controlled-Fourier gate described in the main text. (c) An optical cir- cuit implementing conditional rotations through the dispersive inter- actions between a single atom in a cavity and traveling waves. Two circulators route the CV mode around the loop, and a time-dependent optical switch redirects the beams onto a cavity containing an atom to implement the noise...

FIG. 2. Multiqubit extension for converting thermal or Gaussian displacement noise, assumed to be solely between the suppression unitaries, into a mixture of Fock-diagonal noise. For pure photon loss, this setup furnishes a coherent Fock damping on the input so that the output is an attenuated pure state.

FIG. 3. Average suppression performances as a function of the pho- ton loss rate η for varying numbers of DV ancillas K. The solid lines (K = 1, 2, 3) demonstrate rapid convergence toward the theoretical asymptotic limit. Perfect ancillas have been assumed in order to fo- cus on the effects of adding the ancillas alone here.

FIG. 4. Comparison of average suppression performance with conditional Fourier (CF) gates between a like (even)-parity code bin(2, 4) and an opposite-parity bosonic code: cat(6, 1.916), both of which have similar average Gaussian moments of the photon- number distribution (⟨a†a⟩∼= 4, ⟨(a†a)2⟩∼= 20, ⟨a2⟩= 0) with respect to the state CL. The former is more resilient to the compos- ite amplitude and phase damping qubit noise of equal strengths p. The dashed and dot-dashed lines represent their respective, roughly identical performances without suppression. These are flat because no ancillas were used. Insets show average success probability.

FIG. 6. Average infidelity of the eigenstates of the single-qubit Paulis encoded on the two-component cat code with coherent ampli- tude of 2 or cat(2,2) and the same with an additional 6dB of squeez- ing along the real axis applied to the cat codewords, sqcat(2,6dB), with our suppression protocol with a single DV ancilla affected by various damping noise strengths and without suppression. The CV noise is characterized by the loss parameter µ and the gain parameter z = 1 −1/G. The squeezed cat code retains its advantage over the bare cat code after suppression, demonstrating that active squeezing and the present CF-based protocol can be used in tandem.
